steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Wade argues that three pieces in Heege's manuscript came from a minstrel working in Heege's local area. His existing evidence (addressing audiences, simple rhyming, requests for payment) proves the pieces were meant for live performance — but not that the minstrel was local. A supporting statement must close that gap by establishing a regional tie.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — The three pieces in question contain references that presume that the audience is familiar with Brackonwet and other small villages near where Heege lived.: References to specific nearby villages show the pieces were crafted for a local audience in Heege's region — directly supporting the claim that they came from a local minstrel's repertoire. ✓
  • Choice B — Features like simple rhyming also occur in other texts from the period that were widely read but are not known to have been performed for live audiences.: This weakens Wade's argument by suggesting the performance features aren't reliable indicators. ✗
  • Choice C — Heege's collection contains the earliest examples of the three pieces in question, but each of those pieces occurs in other manuscript collections compiled after Heege's.: Wide circulation suggests the pieces weren't tied to a local minstrel — this undermines the regional origin claim. ✗
  • Choice D — Traveling minstrels are thought to have performed mainly for elite audiences, and other parts of Heege's collection reflect his interest in works with popular appeal.: This addresses Heege's tastes, not the geographic origin of the minstrel. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
